title: Glasses Cleaning Kit: What You Need and What to Avoid
description: The best glasses cleaning kit includes microfiber cloth, lens spray, and a case. Learn what to use, what damages lenses, and how to clean glasses properly.
tldr: A good glasses cleaning kit needs just three things: a microfiber cloth, alcohol-free lens cleaning spray, and a hard-shell case. Avoid paper towels, household glass cleaner, and your shirt — they all scratch lens coatings. Clean your glasses daily with the right tools and they'll last years longer.

tldrStats.label: All you need in a cleaning kit
Cost of a quality cleaning kit

definition.text: A set of tools designed to safely clean prescription eyeglasses without damaging lens coatings. Typically includes a microfiber cleaning cloth, lens cleaning solution (alcohol-free), and a protective case. Some kits add a lens cleaning pen or disposable wipes.

sections.h2: What Should Be in a Glasses Cleaning Kit?
sections.content:
You don't need anything fancy. A proper glasses cleaning kit contains:

  • Microfiber cloth: The single most important item. These cloths have ultra-fine fibers that trap dust and oil without scratching. Every pair of glasses should come with one.
  • Lens cleaning spray: Look for alcohol-free, ammonia-free formulas. These are safe for all lens coatings including anti-reflective, blue light, and hydrophobic treatments.
  • Hard-shell case: Protects glasses when you're not wearing them. Soft pouches work for quick storage, but a rigid case prevents the frame from getting bent or lenses from getting scratched.

Optional but useful additions:

  • Lens cleaning wipes (individually wrapped): Great for your bag, desk, or car. Convenient when you can't rinse your glasses first.
  • Ultrasonic cleaner: A small device that uses vibrations in water to deep-clean frames. Nice for removing buildup around nose pads and hinges. Costs $20-$50.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: How to Clean Glasses the Right Way
sections.content:
The proper method takes about 30 seconds and protects your coatings:

  1. Rinse under lukewarm water. This washes away dust and grit that would scratch if you wiped dry. Use lukewarm — hot water can damage lens coatings.
  2. Apply a small drop of dish soap or lens spray. Plain dish soap (Dawn, Palmolive) works perfectly. Rub gently on both sides of each lens with your fingertips.
  3. Rinse again thoroughly. Make sure all soap residue is gone.
  4. Shake off excess water. A gentle shake removes most droplets.
  5. Dry with a clean microfiber cloth. Use gentle circular motions. Make sure the cloth itself is clean — a dirty microfiber can scratch.

If you can't rinse (you're at your desk, in the car), spray lens cleaner on both sides and wipe with a microfiber cloth. The spray lubricates the surface so particles don't drag across the coating.

sections.h2: What Damages Glasses (Things to Never Use)
sections.content:
More glasses get ruined by bad cleaning habits than by accidental drops. Avoid these:

  • Paper towels and tissues: Feel soft but contain wood fibers that create micro-scratches on lens coatings. Over time, these scratches accumulate and cause a hazy, cloudy appearance.
  • Your shirt or clothing: Fabric texture varies. Cotton t-shirts might seem fine, but dust trapped in the fabric acts like sandpaper. Plus clothing detergent residue can leave a film.
  • Household glass cleaner (Windex): Contains ammonia that strips anti-reflective and hydrophobic coatings. One application might not visibly damage them, but repeated use will.
  • Hand sanitizer: High alcohol concentration degrades lens coatings.
  • Hot water: Temperatures above 40C (100F) can warp lens coatings and even frame materials.
  • Saliva: Yes, people actually do this. It doesn't clean well and introduces bacteria.

If your lenses already look hazy from coating damage, an optician can evaluate whether new lenses are needed or if a professional cleaning might help.

sections.h2: Best Glasses Cleaning Kits You Can Buy
sections.content:
Most optical stores sell cleaning kits, and many include one free with your glasses purchase. Here's what to look for at different price points:

  • Basic kit ($10-$15): Microfiber cloth + lens spray bottle. All most people need.
  • Travel kit ($15-$20): Compact spray, small microfiber, plus a pack of individually wrapped lens wipes. Good for keeping in your bag.
  • Premium kit ($20-$35): Larger spray bottle, multiple microfiber cloths, wipes, and sometimes a small ultrasonic cleaner or lens pen.

Your local optical store is often the best place to pick up cleaning supplies. They'll know which products are safe for the specific coatings on your lenses. Stores like Charm Optical typically stock cleaning kits at the counter.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: How Often Should You Clean Your Glasses?
sections.content:
Clean your glasses at least once daily — ideally in the morning before you start your day. If you work in a dusty environment, cook frequently, or exercise with your glasses on, clean them more often.

Beyond daily cleaning:

  • Weekly: Do a thorough rinse-and-soap cleaning rather than just a quick wipe
  • Monthly: Clean the nose pads and hinges where oil and dead skin accumulate. A soft toothbrush with dish soap works well for this
  • Every 3-6 months: Bring your glasses to your optician for a professional ultrasonic cleaning and adjustment. Most stores offer this free

Replace your microfiber cloth when it starts feeling less effective — usually every 3-6 months with regular use. Machine wash them without fabric softener (softener leaves a residue that transfers to lenses).

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

sections.h2: When to Visit Your Optician for Professional Help
sections.content:
While many glasses issues can be handled at home, some problems require professional tools and expertise. Bent metal frames need careful adjustment with pliers designed specifically for eyewear — using household tools risks snapping the frame. Nose pad replacement requires the exact size and shape for your frame model. And any adjustment that changes how the lenses sit in front of your eyes can affect your vision quality.

Most optical stores offer free adjustments for glasses purchased from them. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians will adjust your frames, replace nose pads, tighten screws, and check your lens alignment at no charge — even if your glasses weren't purchased there. This kind of ongoing service is one of the biggest advantages of buying from a local optical store versus ordering online.

If your lenses are scratched, foggy, or the coatings are peeling, it's time for new lenses rather than a repair. Many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical at Charm Optical, and their direct insurance billing means you can use your benefits toward the replacement. book an eye exam online to get your prescription checked while you're there — it may have changed since your last visit.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What is the best thing to clean glasses with?
faqs.a: Lukewarm water, a drop of plain dish soap, and a clean microfiber cloth. This combination is safe for all lens coatings, effective at removing oils and smudges, and costs almost nothing. For on-the-go cleaning, use an alcohol-free lens spray with a microfiber cloth.

faqs.q: Can I use alcohol wipes on glasses?
faqs.a: Avoid high-concentration alcohol wipes (like hand sanitizer wipes). They can degrade anti-reflective and hydrophobic coatings over time. Lens-specific wipes from optical brands are formulated to be safe — check that they're designed for coated lenses.

faqs.q: How do I clean my glasses without scratching them?
faqs.a: Always rinse with water first to remove dust particles. Never wipe dry lenses — spray lens cleaner first. Use only microfiber cloths, never paper towels, tissues, or clothing. Keep your microfiber cloth clean by washing it weekly.

faqs.q: Is it OK to use Windex on glasses?
faqs.a: No. Windex and similar household glass cleaners contain ammonia, which strips the anti-reflective, hydrophobic, and other coatings from prescription lenses. Stick to dedicated lens cleaning spray or plain dish soap and water.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
faqs.a: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.
